text: The surgeon mobilizes the stomach and creates a window in the gastrocolic ligament, providing access to the lesser sac.
The surgeon sutures the stomach to the interior abdominal wall, securing it for retraction during the jejunostomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies the pancreas, which appears grossly abnormal in appearance.
The surgeon uses intraoperative ultrasound to visualize the pancreatic duct and identify stones within the duct.
The surgeon opens the pancreatic duct along its length using monopolar electrocautery, revealing the duct's interior.
Several stones are visible within the pancreatic duct and are carefully removed using surgical instruments.
The surgeon opens the pancreatic duct distally towards the tail and proceeds to remove it proximally, revealing the duct's interior along its length.

An intraoperative ultrasound is performed to identify the location of the gastrogluteal artery in the neck of the pancreas, providing a visual assessment of the artery's position relative to the pancreatic tissue.
The surgeon creates a window in the transverse mesentery to bring the Roux limb up to the lesser sac, facilitating the jejunostomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies the proximal jejunum at the ligament of Treitz and brings it through the mesenteric window.
The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealing device to divide the mesentery, separating it into distinct sections.
The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to divide the jejunum, completing the separation of this section of the intestine.
The surgeon performs a side-to-side enteroenterostomy by placing two anchoring sutures, one proximal and one distal, under the planned anastomosis site.
The surgeon creates two enterotomies using electrocautery and performs a side-to-side stapled enteroenterostomy, joining the bowel segments with a stapler.
The surgeon closes the enterotomy created by the staple using a barbed monofilament absorbable suture, ensuring the edges of the jejunal tissue are approximated securely.
The surgeon brings the rural limb through the mesenteric defect and positions it adjacent to the pancreas, ensuring proper alignment for the jejunostomy procedure.
The surgeon creates an enterotomy in the jejunum using electrocautery, preparing the site for further surgical manipulation.
The surgeon carefully matches the length of the enterotomy with the opening of the pancreatic duct, ensuring precise alignment between the two structures.
The surgeon creates a longitudinal pancreatic vaginostomy using monofilament absorbable suture, starting at the apex of the pancreatic tail.
The surgeon begins running the posterior portion of the anastomosis, carefully approximating the serosa of the jejunum with the cut edge of the pancreas to ensure a secure connection.
The surgeon proceeds with the anterior layer of the anastomosis between the jejunum and pancreas, using monofilament absorbable suture to ensure precise approximation of the serosal surfaces.
The surgeon ensures good approximation between the serosa of the jejunum and the pancreatic edge, carefully inverting the edges of the mucosa during the anastomosis.
Near the completion of the anastomosis, the surgeon carefully matches the lengths of the jejunal and pancreatic openings to prevent a dog ear deformity at the corner of the anastomosis.
At the completion of the anastomosis, the surgeon sprays fibrin sealant over the pancreatic digenostomy site, ensuring coverage of the newly formed junction.
The surgeon releases the stomach from retraction, allowing it to return to its natural position after completing the anastomosis.
